name: Mindfulness
shortDescription: Mindfulness, or *sati* in Pali, is the practice of paying attention to the present moment without judgment. It is a core component of Buddhist meditation and a path to insight and liberation.
description: Mindfulness, in the context of Buddhist mythology and practice, is far more than simple awareness. It's a deliberate and sustained focus on the present moment, encompassing bodily sensations, thoughts, emotions, and external stimuli, all observed without judgment or attachment. This cultivation of *sati* (Pali) or *smṛti* (Sanskrit) is not a passive state; it's an active engagement with experience as it unfolds. It is a cornerstone of the Noble Eightfold Path, specifically Right Mindfulness, and is considered essential for developing wisdom and compassion. Through consistent mindfulness practice, practitioners aim to dismantle habitual patterns of reactivity, understand the impermanent nature of reality (anicca), and ultimately, alleviate suffering. Mindfulness is integral to both Theravada and Mahayana Buddhist traditions. In Theravada, it often involves focused attention on breath, body scans, and mindful walking. In Mahayana, it can be incorporated into various practices, including Zen meditation (zazen) and mindful engagement in daily activities. The development of mindfulness is closely linked to the other factors of the Eightfold Path, especially Right Concentration (samadhi) and Right Understanding (prajna). Through mindfulness, one observes the arising and passing away of phenomena, leading to a deeper understanding of the interconnectedness of all things and the nature of suffering (dukkha). It is a crucial step on the bodhisattva path, enabling individuals to cultivate compassion and wisdom for the benefit of all beings.

sources.title: The Heart of the Buddha's Teaching
sources.author: Thich Nhat Hanh
